abstract: Astrophysicists have long utilized chemical models to explore the processes governing the chemical composition of gas and dust in the interstellar medium (ISM). Recently, the predictive power of these models has improved significantly due to advancements in experimental techniques and theoretical methods. These developments allow researchers to study individual reaction rate constants under conditions that closely mimic the extreme environments of the ISM. Furthermore, because chemical compositions in star-forming regions are far from a steady state, contemporary models increasingly incorporate the dynamical evolution of physical conditions. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the state of the art in chemical modeling for dark clouds—the critical initial stage in the formation of stars and planetary disks. Combining insights from current kinetic databases and reaction networks, we assess how well these models reproduce observed molecular abundances and discuss the ongoing challenges in bridging the gap between theory and observation.
